lessons.title: What systemd is: the manager of background programs
lessons.content:
## The program that runs your other programs

You already met systemd in the boot chain: it is the **first program** the Linux kernel starts (process number 1), and it stays running the whole time the machine is on. But what does it actually *do* all day?

**Think of systemd as a building manager.** Every morning the manager unlocks the building and opens each shop in the right order. If a shop's lights suddenly go out, the manager notices and reopens it. systemd does the same for the **background programs** on your computer.

Those background programs have a name: a **service**, also called a **daemon** (pronounced "demon"). A service is just a program that runs quietly in the background instead of in a window you look at — things like the web server, the part that lets you log in over the network (`sshd`), or your printing system. They have no buttons; they wait and do their job.

So systemd's whole job is to:

- **Start** services when the machine boots, in the correct order.
- **Stop** and **restart** them when asked.
- **Watch** them, and **restart** one automatically if it crashes.
- **Collect their logs** in one place so you can see what happened.

You don't talk to systemd directly. You use one command, **`systemctl`** ("system control"), to tell it what to do, and another, **`journalctl`**, to read the logs.

**Recap:** systemd is PID 1, the always-on manager that starts, stops, supervises, and restarts the background programs (services / daemons) on a modern Linux system.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is systemd's main job on a Linux system?
choices: To start, stop, supervise, and restart the background programs (services) on the machine
To edit text files for you
To draw the desktop and windows on screen
To connect the computer to Wi-Fi networks only
items.explanation: systemd is the system/service manager: it brings services up at boot, lets you control them, and restarts crashed ones. Drawing windows and editing files are jobs of other programs; networking is handled by services that systemd may manage, but that is not its whole job.

items.prompt: What is a "service" (also called a "daemon")?
choices: A program that runs quietly in the background with no window, waiting to do its job
A physical button on the computer case
A type of cable that connects to the network
The user account you log in with
items.explanation: A service/daemon is a background program (a web server, ssh, printing, etc.) that has no window and keeps running to provide something. "Daemon" is just the traditional Unix word for it; it is not hardware or an account.

items.prompt: From the boot-chain lesson: which process is systemd, and when does it start?
choices: It is process number 1 (PID 1), the first program the kernel starts, and it keeps running while the machine is on
It is the last program to start, just before shutdown
It only starts when you open a terminal
It runs inside the web browser
items.explanation: The kernel hands off to systemd as PID 1, the first userspace program, and systemd stays alive the entire session. It is not tied to terminals or browsers, and it starts at the very beginning, not the end.

items.prompt: The lesson compares systemd to a building manager. What does that analogy capture best?
choices: It opens each shop in the right order each morning and reopens any that close unexpectedly
It charges rent to each program
It decides what color the building is painted
It only works on weekends
items.explanation: The point of the analogy is ordering at startup plus automatic recovery: open things in the correct order, and reopen (restart) anything that goes down. The other options are not part of the analogy.

items.prompt: Which command do you use to TELL systemd to start, stop, or check a service?
choices: systemctl
journalctl
notepad
ifconfig
items.explanation: systemctl ("system control") is the command you use to control services. journalctl is for reading logs, not controlling services; the others are unrelated.

items.prompt: One of systemd's jobs is supervision. What does that mean in practice?
choices: It watches running services and can restart one automatically if it crashes
It asks you for permission before every action
It deletes any program it does not recognize
It runs each service exactly once and never again
items.explanation: Supervision means systemd keeps an eye on services and can bring a crashed one back up automatically. It does not delete unknown programs, and it can run a service many times (including restarts).

items.prompt: Where do the messages (logs) from services end up by default on a systemd machine?
choices: In one central log called the journal, which you read with journalctl
In a popup window for each message
They are thrown away immediately and cannot be read
Printed on paper by the printer service
items.explanation: systemd collects service output into a central journal that you read with journalctl. Logs are kept (at least for the current boot), not discarded, and certainly not printed.

items.prompt: Why is it useful that ONE manager (systemd) handles all services instead of each service fending for itself?
choices: You get one consistent way to start/stop/check every service, ordering between them, automatic restarts, and one place for logs
It makes the computer use less electricity
It removes the need to ever update software
It guarantees no program can ever crash
items.explanation: A single manager gives you uniform control, dependency ordering, automatic recovery, and centralized logging. It does not stop crashes from happening (it just reacts to them), nor does it affect power use or updates.

items.prompt: Long ago, the old init system started services by running shell scripts strictly one after another. What is one beginner-level improvement systemd brought?
choices: It can start independent services at the same time and restart them if they crash, instead of just running scripts in a fixed line
It made shell scripts impossible to write
It removed the idea of background programs entirely
It requires every service to have a graphical window
items.explanation: systemd replaced the old strictly-sequential script approach with parallel startup, ordering by stated needs, and ongoing supervision (restart on crash). Scripts still exist; services are still background programs without windows.

lessons.title: Driving services with systemctl (start, stop, enable)
lessons.content:
## The everyday commands

Now that you know systemd manages services, here is how you actually drive it. You use one command, **`systemctl`**, followed by a verb (what to do) and the service name. Service names usually end in `.service`.

Start with the safest verb — just *looking*:

```bash
systemctl status sshd.service
```

This shows whether the service is running, whether it's set to start at boot, and the last few log lines. It changes nothing.

The core control verbs:

```bash
sudo systemctl start sshd     # run it NOW
sudo systemctl stop sshd      # stop it NOW
sudo systemctl restart sshd   # stop then start (apply changes)
```

(`sudo` is needed because changing system services requires admin rights. You can usually drop the `.service` ending.)

**The one distinction beginners must get right: `enable` vs `start`.**

- **`start`** runs the service **right now**, this one time. Reboot, and it won't come back on its own.
- **`enable`** marks the service to **start automatically at every boot**. By itself, `enable` does *not* start it now.

```bash
sudo systemctl enable sshd        # auto-start at boot (not now)
sudo systemctl disable sshd       # stop auto-starting at boot
sudo systemctl enable --now sshd  # do BOTH: enable at boot AND start now
```

Think of a shop: **`start`** is unlocking the door today; **`enable`** is putting it on the manager's "open this every morning" list. Most of the time you want both, which is why `enable --now` exists.

**Recap:** `status` to look, `start`/`stop`/`restart` to control it now, `enable`/`disable` to decide whether it comes up at boot. enable = at boot; start = now.

items.prompt: You want to make a service run RIGHT NOW, just this once. Which verb?
choices: systemctl start
systemctl enable
systemctl disable
systemctl mask
items.explanation: start runs the service immediately. enable only schedules it for future boots, disable removes that scheduling, and mask blocks it entirely — none of those start it now.

items.prompt: Which command makes a service start automatically every time the computer boots — the one that makes it run at boot?
choices: systemctl enable
systemctl start
systemctl status
systemctl restart
items.explanation: enable is the verb that wires a service into the boot process so it auto-starts on every boot. start only runs it once now; status just looks; restart cycles a running service.

items.prompt: You run `sudo systemctl start myapp`, confirm it's running, then reboot. After reboot, myapp is NOT running. Why?
choices: start only runs it once; you never ran enable, so it does not come back at boot
Rebooting permanently deletes services
start always turns a service off after a reboot on purpose
You must run start twice for it to stick
items.explanation: start affects only the current session. To survive a reboot you must enable the service (or use enable --now). The service was not deleted, and start does not need to be run twice.

items.prompt: What does `sudo systemctl enable --now sshd` do?
choices: Enables it to start at every boot AND starts it right now
Only starts it now, with no effect on future boots
Only schedules it for boot, without starting it now
Permanently blocks the service from ever running
items.explanation: The --now flag combines enable (auto-start at boot) with start (run immediately), which is what you usually want. Without --now, enable would skip starting it now.

items.prompt: Which command just shows you whether a service is running and its recent log lines, without changing anything?
choices: systemctl status myapp
systemctl stop myapp
systemctl disable myapp
systemctl restart myapp
items.explanation: status is read-only: it reports active/inactive, enabled/disabled, and recent logs. stop, disable, and restart all change the service's state.

items.prompt: A service is running and you want it to STOP running this instant (but you don't care about boot settings). Which verb?
choices: systemctl stop
systemctl disable
systemctl enable
systemctl status
items.explanation: stop halts the running service now. disable only changes whether it starts at the next boot — a disabled service that is currently running keeps running until you stop it.

items.prompt: Using the shop analogy from the lesson, what is the difference between start and enable?
choices: start = unlock the shop today; enable = put it on the 'open every morning' list
start = paint the shop; enable = hire staff
start = close forever; enable = open today
They mean exactly the same thing
items.explanation: start is a one-time 'open it now,' while enable adds it to the recurring 'open at every boot' list. They are different actions, which is why both exist (and combine as enable --now).

items.prompt: Why do start/stop/enable on system services usually need `sudo`?
choices: Changing system-wide services requires administrator rights
sudo makes the commands run faster
systemctl will not type without sudo
sudo is only needed to read logs
items.explanation: System services affect the whole machine, so changing them is an administrator action that needs sudo. It is about permission, not speed, and reading status often does not need sudo at all.

items.prompt: You currently have a service running and you want it to STOP coming back on future boots, while leaving it running for now. Which single verb fits best?
choices: systemctl disable (it removes boot auto-start but does not stop the running copy)
systemctl stop (it would stop it now too)
systemctl start
systemctl enable
items.explanation: disable removes the auto-start-at-boot setting but does not touch the currently running process, so it keeps running until stopped or the next boot. stop would end it now; enable/start do the opposite of what you want.

items.prompt: What does `systemctl restart myapp` do?
choices: Stops the service and starts it again — commonly used to apply new settings
Permanently disables the service
Only enables it for boot
Shows the logs and exits
items.explanation: restart is stop followed by start, which is how you make a running service pick up changes. It does not disable the service or merely show logs.

lessons.title: Writing a simple .service unit file
lessons.content:
## Describing your own service

So far you've controlled services someone else set up. Now you'll create one. To make systemd manage *your* program, you write a small text file called a **unit file** that describes it. For a service, the file ends in `.service`.

A unit file is plain text in sections marked with `[Brackets]`. Here is a complete, minimal example for a program at `/usr/bin/myapp`:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=My App

[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/bin/myapp
Restart=on-failure
User=myapp

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Let's read it line by line:

- **`[Unit]` / `Description=`** — a human-friendly name that shows up in `systemctl status`.
- **`[Service]`** — how to run the program:
  - **`ExecStart=`** is the most important line: the exact command to launch. Use a full path.
  - **`Restart=on-failure`** tells systemd to restart the program only if it crashes (exits with an error), but not if you stopped it on purpose. This is the supervision you learned about.
  - **`User=myapp`** runs the program as an ordinary user instead of all-powerful root, so a bug or break-in can do less damage.
- **`[Install]` / `WantedBy=multi-user.target`** — this is read only when you `enable` the service. It tells systemd where to hook the service in at boot. (`multi-user.target` is roughly "the normal, fully-booted state"; more on targets later.) Without an `[Install]` section you can still `start` the service by hand, but you cannot `enable` it.

**Where to put it, and the gotcha:** save your own unit files in **`/etc/systemd/system/`** (for example `/etc/systemd/system/myapp.service`). After you create or edit any unit file, systemd hasn't read it yet — you must run:

```bash
sudo systemctl daemon-reload     # re-read unit files from disk
sudo systemctl enable --now myapp
```

Forgetting `daemon-reload` after editing is the classic beginner trap: systemd keeps using the old version until you reload.

**Recap:** a `.service` file in `/etc/systemd/system/` describes your program with `ExecStart=`, `Restart=`, and `User=`; `[Install] WantedBy=` lets you enable it; always `daemon-reload` after editing.

items.prompt: Which line in a .service file gives the actual command systemd should run to start the program?
choices: ExecStart=
Description=
WantedBy=
User=
items.explanation: ExecStart= is the command to launch (use a full path). Description= is just a label, WantedBy= controls boot wiring, and User= picks which account it runs as — none of those launch the program.

items.prompt: After you create or edit a unit file on disk, which command makes systemd actually re-read it?
choices: sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl status
sudo systemctl mask
sudo reboot
items.explanation: daemon-reload tells systemd to re-read unit files from disk. Without it, systemd keeps using the old version. status only displays info; mask blocks a unit; rebooting works but is overkill and not the intended tool.

items.prompt: What does `Restart=on-failure` do?
choices: Restarts the program if it crashes (exits with an error), but not when you stop it on purpose
Restarts the whole computer when the program fails
Prevents the program from ever restarting
Restarts the program every 5 minutes no matter what
items.explanation: on-failure restarts only on an abnormal exit, and respects a deliberate stop. It does not reboot the machine, does not run on a fixed timer, and clearly does allow restarts.

items.prompt: Why is it good practice to set `User=myapp` instead of running the service as root?
choices: Running as an ordinary user limits the damage if the program has a bug or is broken into
It makes the program run faster
root is not allowed to run services at all
It hides the service from systemctl status
items.explanation: Dropping to a non-root user is least-privilege: a compromised or buggy service can do far less. It is not about speed, root can run services, and the service still shows in status.

items.prompt: Where should you save your own custom unit files?
choices: /etc/systemd/system/
/home/Documents/
Anywhere, systemd searches the whole disk
Inside the program's own folder only
items.explanation: Admin-created and custom units belong in /etc/systemd/system/. systemd looks in specific directories, not the whole disk, and not your home or the app folder.

items.prompt: What is the role of the `[Install]` section with `WantedBy=multi-user.target`?
choices: It tells systemd where to hook the service in at boot; it is read when you run enable
It is the command that runs the program
It sets the user the program runs as
It deletes the service after one run
items.explanation: [Install] is consumed by enable to wire the unit into a boot target so it auto-starts. The command is ExecStart=, the user is User=, and nothing here deletes the service.

items.prompt: You wrote a unit file with no `[Install]` section. What happens?
choices: You can still start it by hand, but you cannot enable it (there is nowhere to hook it in at boot)
systemd refuses to load the file at all
It will start at boot automatically anyway
It can be enabled but never started
items.explanation: Without [Install], enable has no WantedBy target to create the boot link, so enable fails — but manual start still works. The file is otherwise valid and will not auto-start.

items.prompt: You edited myapp.service but your change seems to have no effect. What is the most likely beginner mistake?
choices: You forgot to run daemon-reload, so systemd is still using the old version of the file
Unit files cannot be edited once created
You must rename the file every time you edit it
Editing a unit file deletes it
items.explanation: systemd caches unit files in memory; after editing you must daemon-reload (and usually restart the service) for changes to take effect. Files can be edited freely and are not renamed or deleted by editing.

items.prompt: In which section does `ExecStart=` belong?
choices: [Service]
[Unit]
[Install]
[Boot]
items.explanation: ExecStart= describes how to run the program, so it lives in [Service]. [Unit] holds metadata like Description=, [Install] holds WantedBy=, and there is no [Boot] section.

items.prompt: After saving a brand-new unit file, what is a correct sequence to load it, run it now, and have it start at boot?
choices: sudo systemctl daemon-reload, then sudo systemctl enable --now myapp
sudo systemctl enable myapp only, nothing else
Just reboot and hope it appears
sudo systemctl mask myapp
items.explanation: daemon-reload makes systemd see the new file, then enable --now both starts it now and wires it for boot. enable alone would not start it now, and mask would block it.

lessons.title: Unit types, targets & overrides
lessons.content:
## Beyond services: types, targets, and safe edits

A `.service` is one **kind** of unit, but systemd manages several. The ending of the unit name tells you its type:

- **`.service`** — a background program (what you've been writing).
- **`.target`** — a named *group* of units used as a milestone. It does no work itself; it just pulls in a set of other units.
- **`.timer`** — runs a service on a schedule (covered next lesson).
- **`.socket`** — listens on a network port and starts a service the moment a connection arrives, so the service doesn't have to run until it's actually needed.

**Targets replace the old "runlevels."** Decades ago, Linux described "how far booted" with numbered runlevels. systemd uses named targets instead, which read more clearly. The common one is **`multi-user.target`**: a fully-booted, networked, multi-user system (no graphical desktop). `graphical.target` adds the desktop. When you `enable` a service with `WantedBy=multi-user.target`, reaching that target at boot pulls your service in — like a checklist the manager completes each morning.

**A note on dependencies (how ordering works):** a unit can say it `Wants=` or `Requires=` another, and `After=` it. Roughly, `Wants=`/`Requires=` decide *whether* another unit is pulled in, and `After=` decides the *order*. As a beginner you mostly rely on the defaults; just know systemd starts independent services in parallel and only waits where you tell it to.

**Editing safely — the override gotcha:** services installed by your distribution live in `/usr/lib/systemd/system/`. **Do not edit those files** — a software update will overwrite your changes. Instead, override just the bits you want:

```bash
sudo systemctl edit sshd.service
```

This creates a small **drop-in** file under `/etc/systemd/system/sshd.service.d/` containing only your changes, which sit *on top of* the vendor file and survive updates. Files in `/etc/systemd/system/` always win over those in `/usr/lib/`. As always, after any hand edit run `sudo systemctl daemon-reload`.

There's also **`mask`**: `systemctl mask foo` makes a unit completely unstartable (stronger than `disable`); `unmask` reverses it.

**Recap:** unit types are named by suffix (`.service/.target/.timer/.socket`); targets are the modern named milestones (`multi-user.target`) that replaced runlevels; edit vendor units with `systemctl edit` drop-ins in `/etc/`, never `/usr/lib/`, then `daemon-reload`.

items.prompt: A unit named `backup.timer` — what type of unit is it, based on its suffix?
choices: A timer, which runs a service on a schedule
A service, which is a background program
A target, which is a boot milestone
A socket, which listens on a port
items.explanation: The suffix after the final dot names the type: .timer is a scheduling unit. .service, .target, and .socket are different types with their own suffixes.

items.prompt: What is a `.target` unit, conceptually?
choices: A named group / milestone that pulls in a set of other units but does no work itself
A background program that supervises other programs
A schedule for running a service nightly
A network port a service listens on
items.explanation: A target is a grouping and synchronization point (like a checklist item) that pulls in other units; it runs nothing on its own. The other descriptions are services, timers, and sockets respectively.

items.prompt: Targets are the modern replacement for what older concept?
choices: Numbered runlevels
Device drivers
User passwords
Disk partitions
items.explanation: systemd targets (e.g. multi-user.target) replaced the old numbered runlevels with clearer named milestones. Drivers, passwords, and partitions are unrelated subsystems.

items.prompt: What does `multi-user.target` roughly represent?
choices: A fully-booted, networked, multi-user system without a graphical desktop
A single-user repair mode with nothing running
The moment the power button is pressed
A screensaver
items.explanation: multi-user.target is the normal fully-booted text/server state; graphical.target adds the desktop on top. It is not an early power-on stage, repair mode, or a screensaver.

items.prompt: You want to change one setting on a service your distribution installed, in a way that survives software updates. What is the recommended approach?
choices: Run `systemctl edit` to create a small drop-in override in /etc/systemd/system/
Edit the vendor file directly in /usr/lib/systemd/system/
Delete the vendor file and rewrite it from scratch
Change the setting by renaming the service
items.explanation: systemctl edit creates a drop-in in /etc that overrides only what you change and is preserved across updates. Editing /usr/lib gets overwritten on update; deleting/renaming the vendor file is fragile and breaks on upgrade.

items.prompt: Why should you NOT edit unit files in /usr/lib/systemd/system/ directly?
choices: A package update will overwrite them, erasing your changes
The files there are encrypted
systemd ignores everything in /usr/lib
Editing them deletes the service
items.explanation: /usr/lib holds vendor (package-provided) files that updates replace, so your edits vanish. The files are readable, systemd does use them as defaults, and editing does not delete anything — the issue is durability.

items.prompt: If the same unit exists in both /etc/systemd/system/ and /usr/lib/systemd/system/, which wins?
choices: The one in /etc/systemd/system/ (admin overrides take precedence)
The one in /usr/lib/systemd/system/ (vendor always wins)
Neither; systemd refuses to start
Whichever is alphabetically first
items.explanation: /etc is for admin overrides and takes precedence over the vendor defaults in /usr/lib. This is exactly why drop-ins and copies in /etc work to override packaged units.

items.prompt: Roughly, what is the difference between `After=` and `Wants=` on a unit?
choices: After= sets the ORDER (start after that unit); Wants= decides WHETHER the other unit is pulled in
They are identical
After= deletes the other unit; Wants= copies it
After= is for timers only; Wants= is for sockets only
items.explanation: Ordering (After=) and requirement/pulling-in (Wants=) are separate ideas. They are not identical, do not delete or copy units, and are not restricted to particular unit types.

items.prompt: Which unit type listens on a network port and starts its service only when a connection actually arrives?
choices: .socket
.target
.timer
.service
items.explanation: A .socket unit holds the listening port and triggers the matching service on first connection (lazy start). Targets are milestones, timers are schedules, and the .service is the program being started.

items.prompt: What does `systemctl mask foo` do, compared to `disable`?
choices: mask makes the unit completely unstartable (even by hand), which is stronger than disable
mask and disable are exactly the same
mask starts the service immediately
mask deletes the unit file from disk
items.explanation: mask links the unit to nothing so it cannot be started at all until unmasked; disable only stops it auto-starting at boot. mask does not start anything and does not delete the original file.

items.prompt: After `systemctl edit` writes a drop-in override, what must you do for systemd to use it?
choices: Run daemon-reload (systemctl edit usually does this for you) and restart the service to apply it
Nothing; overrides apply retroactively to the past
Rename the service
Delete /usr/lib entirely
items.explanation: systemd must re-read units (daemon-reload) and the running service must be restarted to pick up changes. Overrides do not affect the past, and you never need to rename services or wipe /usr/lib.

lessons.title: Timers and reading logs with journalctl
lessons.content:
## Scheduling jobs and finding out what happened

Two very practical skills round out the basics: running jobs on a schedule, and reading logs.

### Timers — the modern cron

If you've heard of **cron**, that's the classic Unix way to run a job on a schedule ("every night at 2:30"). systemd offers **timers** as a tidy replacement. A timer is just a unit ending in `.timer` that triggers a same-named `.service`. So `backup.timer` runs `backup.service`.

```ini
# /etc/systemd/system/backup.timer
[Unit]
Description=Nightly backup

[Timer]
OnCalendar=*-*-* 02:30:00
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
```

- **`OnCalendar=*-*-* 02:30:00`** means "every day at 02:30."
- **`Persistent=true`** means: if the machine was off at 02:30, run the job soon after it next boots, so a missed run isn't simply skipped.

Why prefer timers over cron? The job is a normal service, so it gets restart rules, runs as a chosen `User=`, and — importantly — its output goes to the journal like everything else. List your timers with `systemctl list-timers`.

### journald and journalctl — reading the logs

The service that collects logs is **journald**; you read them with **`journalctl`**. The single most useful command is filtering to one service with `-u`:

```bash
journalctl -u myapp           # all logged lines for myapp
journalctl -u myapp -f        # follow live, like tail -f
journalctl -u myapp -b        # only since the last boot
```

- **`-u NAME`** = just this unit's logs (the answer to "where do I read a service's logs?").
- **`-f`** = keep showing new lines as they arrive (great while testing).
- **`-b`** = limit to the current boot; `-b -1` shows the previous boot.

### One line on hardening

When you write a service, you can add cheap safety directives in `[Service]`, such as `NoNewPrivileges=true` (the service can't gain extra powers) and `ProtectSystem=strict` (most of the filesystem becomes read-only to it). You don't need them to start out, but they're easy wins later.

**Recap:** a `.timer` triggers a `.service` on a schedule (cron replacement, `OnCalendar=` + `Persistent=true`); read any service's logs with `journalctl -u NAME` (add `-f` to follow, `-b` for this boot); add `NoNewPrivileges=`/`ProtectSystem=` to harden.

items.prompt: Where do you read the logs for a specific service called myapp?
choices: journalctl -u myapp
systemctl start myapp
cat /myapp.log
systemctl enable myapp
items.explanation: journalctl -u myapp shows just that unit's logs from the journal. systemctl start/enable control the service rather than show logs, and services log to the journal, not a fixed /myapp.log file by default.

items.prompt: What does the `-f` flag do in `journalctl -u myapp -f`?
choices: Follows the log live, showing new lines as they arrive (like tail -f)
Deletes the log file
Shows only errors
Forces the service to start
items.explanation: -f follows the journal, streaming new entries as they appear — handy while testing. It does not delete logs, filter to errors, or start services.

items.prompt: A `.timer` unit named `backup.timer` triggers which service by default?
choices: backup.service (the service with the same base name)
timer.service
all .service units at once
no service; timers run code directly
items.explanation: By default a timer triggers the service sharing its base name, so backup.timer runs backup.service. A timer itself runs no code; it just starts its paired service.

items.prompt: systemd timers are presented as the modern replacement for which classic tool?
choices: cron
grep
ssh
the bootloader
items.explanation: Timers replace cron for scheduling jobs, with the bonus of journald logging and service features. grep, ssh, and the bootloader are unrelated tools.

items.prompt: What does `Persistent=true` add to a timer?
choices: If the machine was off at the scheduled time, the job runs soon after the next boot instead of being skipped
It makes the timer fire every second
It keeps the computer from ever shutting down
It stores the job's output forever
items.explanation: Persistent=true catches up a missed run after downtime. It does not change the firing frequency, prevent shutdown, or control log retention.

items.prompt: Which command lists the timers on your system and their next run times?
choices: systemctl list-timers
journalctl -t
systemctl mask
timer --list
items.explanation: systemctl list-timers shows active timers with their last and next run times. The other options are not the right command for this.

items.prompt: One advantage of a systemd timer over a plain cron job is that the scheduled job's output:
choices: Goes into the journal, where you can read it with journalctl -u
Is permanently hidden from you
Is emailed to every user
Cannot be logged at all
items.explanation: Because a timer triggers a normal service, its output lands in the journal and is readable with journalctl -u. Cron traditionally has weaker, scattered logging; the output is not hidden or unloggable.

items.prompt: You want to see only the log messages from the CURRENT boot for myapp. Which flag combination?
choices: journalctl -u myapp -b
journalctl -u myapp -f
journalctl --all-history
journalctl -u myapp --delete
items.explanation: -b limits output to the current boot (and -b -1 would be the previous boot). -f follows live without limiting to a boot, and the other options are not valid ways to scope by boot.

items.prompt: In `OnCalendar=*-*-* 02:30:00`, what schedule does this describe?
choices: Every day at 02:30 (2:30 AM)
Once, on a single fixed date
Every 2 minutes and 30 seconds
Only on the 2nd and 30th of the month
items.explanation: The asterisks mean 'any year-month-day,' so the time 02:30:00 fires daily at 2:30 AM. It is not a one-off date, an interval, or specific day numbers.

items.prompt: What does a hardening directive like `NoNewPrivileges=true` in a service do?
choices: Prevents the service from gaining extra privileges while running, limiting damage from a compromise
Gives the service full root powers
Makes the service start faster
Schedules the service to run nightly
items.explanation: NoNewPrivileges=true blocks the service from escalating its powers, a cheap safety win. It does the opposite of granting root, has nothing to do with speed, and is unrelated to scheduling.

items.prompt: You just set up myapp.service and want to watch its log live while you test it. Best command?
choices: journalctl -u myapp -f
systemctl enable myapp
journalctl -b -1
systemctl list-timers
items.explanation: journalctl -u myapp -f scopes to your unit and follows new lines as they appear. enable only sets boot behavior, -b -1 shows a past boot, and list-timers shows timers — none follow myapp's live log.
